text:
ThereГўВҖВҷs something different about these Olympics, and itГўВҖВҷs not just E. coli on the triathlon course or a beach volleyball stadium at the foot of the Eiffel Tower. As both a lifelong fan of the Olympics and a media membercovering the Gamesfrom the U.S., I canГўВҖВҷt overstate how much my viewing experience has been enhanced by the emergence ofGold Zone, NBCГўВҖВҷsNFL RedZoneГўВҖВ“style whip-around show taking us around Paris for 10 hours a day.

I also knowIГўВҖВҷm not alone here. If NBC were to pull the plug on this product for future Olympics, some fans would be as angry as if the IOC yanked track and field.

I wrote a little aboutGold Zonebefore the Olympics, in the form ofa Q&A with Scott Hanson, one of the programГўВҖВҷs four daily hosts. I wasnГўВҖВҷt planning on writing about it again, but then I consumed so much of the show that I felt like I couldnГўВҖВҷt go 16 days without singing its praises.

ГўВҖВңIf at the end of the Games, people tell me,That felt like RedZone. Or,That gave me some RedZoneГўВҖВ“esque thrills, that will be mission accomplished,ГўВҖВқ Hanson told me in May. ГўВҖВңIf casual Olympics fans tell me at the end of the Games that they watched more Olympics than they ever have because ofGold Zone, that will be mission accomplished.ГўВҖВқ

Well,mission accomplished.

Gold Zoneis not actually new, but this is the Olympics in which NBC has perfected it. Thanks to better technology, a greater acceptance of watching live sports on streaming platforms and a better time zone, this has been the Olympics when the show broke through.

The program has done many things right, and those four hosts (Hanson, Andrew Siciliano, Matt Iseman and Jac Collinsworth) belong on that list. Each has brought their own cadence to the show, and the regular daily handoffs keep the proceedings from getting monotonousГўВҖВ”especially for those of us who just keep the show running all day.

I figured who better to explain the success of the show than one of those hosts, so I connected with Iseman, who tag-teams the first time slot of the day with Collinsworth. What I got when Iseman picked up the phone was ГўВҖВҰ both Iseman and Collinsworth on speakerphone. ItГўВҖВҷs unclear when theyГўВҖВҷve last left each otherГўВҖВҷs side.

The two hosts, who have spent the past 14 days encouraging fans to tweet photos of themselves watchingGold Zone, shared with me the routine of howtheywatchGold Zone. Their daily ritual: They get off set, grab a bite to eat and then sit on a balcony in their Stamford, Conn., hotel and watch Siciliano and Hanson.

ГўВҖВңNot for research,ГўВҖВқ Iseman says. ГўВҖВңBut because weГўВҖВҷre fans of this. Welovewatching it.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңI think itГўВҖВҷs probably the most fun [time] that IГўВҖВҷve had on TV,ГўВҖВқ Collinsworth says. The pair were relatively fresh off the air and clearly still in a post-show buzz. They sounded just like they do on camera, jumping in to build off each otherГўВҖВҷs points and bursting with nuggets to share.

ГўВҖВңWeГўВҖВҷve both been saying, you will never watch the Olympics the same way again,ГўВҖВқ Iseman adds.

I think heГўВҖВҷs right.

What hooked me onGold Zonefrom the jump is that the show matches my sensibilities exactly. IГўВҖВҷve always thought outlets covering the Olympics could do a better job leaning into the more niche sports. People love it when a dressage horse or a badminton rally goes viral. But NBCГўВҖВҷs prime-time coverage still focuses so much on the biggest sports and most famous athletes, with maybe a few highlights from elsewhere.Gold ZoneГўВҖВҷs multiscreen capabilities and longer runtime lend space to invest a bit of time just to see what might happenГўВҖВ”as well as the good sense to know you canГўВҖВҷt pull away when a wild ending in a France vs. Germany handball game falls in your lap. The showГўВҖВҷs real secret sauce is how it figured out that mix.

Gold Zoneprides itself on showing you every medal. This was made clear during ГўВҖВңGold Rush Weekend,ГўВҖВқ Days 8 and 9, when the hosts kept returning to a graphic that ticked off 49 medal events as they happened over 48 hours (it was 51 before bad weather in Tahiti delayed the surfing). But an event doesnГўВҖВҷt have to be for a medal to capture the attention of our Olympics concierges. ItГўВҖВҷs been fun watching them seamlessly glide from fencing to archery to canoe slalom, sometimes learning the rules right along with viewers, without missing any of the truly crucial moments in swimming or gymnastics.

The show is also different at different times of day. Iseman and Collinsworth captain a true morning show. They appear at 7 a.m. to recap the early-morning action you may have missed, then talk about their coffee and show photos of pets watchingGold Zone. Siciliano and Hanson get more down to business during a time of day when more of the critical medal events seem to air in quick succession. The morning guys have time to fence against each other and bring in kids for a taekwondo demonstration. Hanson often feels like heГўВҖВҷs still living inRedZoneГўВҖВҷs witching hour.

Iseman and Collinsworth come off like longtime buddies, but they had actually never met until three days before the first show. They seem to have become best friends faster than Will Ferrell and John C. Reilly inStep Brothers.

Iseman says much of what you see on air has come together organically. ГўВҖВңOn Friday afternoon [before Day 1], Jac and I were talking, none of us knew what the show would be. You have an idea, but nobodyГўВҖВҷs tried to simultaneously stream up to 40 events at a time, in dozens of different sports, and have it make sense. In our heads, weГўВҖВҷre just picturing, ГўВҖВҳMy God, we might be stuck on speedwalking for an hour.ГўВҖВҷ Within about 15 minutes, we got it.ГўВҖВқ

TheyГўВҖВҷve remained nimble. There was a popular moment on Day 9 when a crew member came on air to break down a controversy in the womenГўВҖВҷs skeet shooting final. Iseman says it was Collinsworth who suggested they put him on air, after observing an animated discussion in the studio.

ГўВҖВңThereГўВҖВҷs no rulebook for this,ГўВҖВқ Collinsworth says. ГўВҖВңThey made a point of telling [us] that. It makes you dip into your creative bag.ГўВҖВқ

Iseman says fans have made the show better, too. ГўВҖВңWe are on Twitter reading the feedback. The quadbox got better because people were saying, ГўВҖВҳHey that logoГўВҖВҷs too big.ГўВҖВҷ We agreed. WeГўВҖВҷve jumped to the six-box, we dumped the decabox.ГўВҖВқ

TheyГўВҖВҷve also gotten more interactive as theyГўВҖВҷve found their audience.

Collinsworth says when the show first started, they were hesitant to promoteGold Zoneas its own hashtag. ГўВҖВңIt was like, ГўВҖВҳNahhh letГўВҖВҷs do #NBCOlympics, so weГўВҖВҷre all on the same thing.ГўВҖВҷ But you could tell, 10 minutes into the first show, we didnГўВҖВҷt have to [ask for] #GoldZone, we were already getting hit with it.ГўВҖВқ

People whoГўВҖВҷve come of age during this era ofRedZoneandManningCastsand putting the Super Bowl on Nickelodeon may not realize what a change this is, or what it used to be like for Olympics fans who wanted midday updates on wrestling and water polo.

Given the relative lack of interest in the Tokyo Games (thanks to a mix of COVID-19 protocols, empty stadiums and a poor time zone), IГўВҖВҷm not sure if everyone quite remembers the extent to which criticizing NBC used to be its own Olympic sport.

I recently went back and read thisWashington Poststory from 2012about how the #NBCFail hashtag went viral. The funny thing is, at the time NBCwasstreaming everything live. I know because I actually worked for NBC during those London Games, a one-month temp position where one of my jobs was to make sure the correct livestream links for the correct judo mats were in the correct spots on the NBCOlympics.com site.

But clearly not everyone knew everything was streaming live, as made clear in that story above. Now,Gold Zonemakes life a lot easier than having to click a link that says, ГўВҖВңRepechage, Finals: M Freestyle 55kg & 74kg.ГўВҖВқ (And Peacock does make it easier to find those types of specific offerings.)

After Peacock hosted multiple NFL games last season, including a Miami Dolphins at Kansas City Chiefs wild-card game, people know. In 2021, NBC angered some fans by putting USA menГўВҖВҷs basketball exclusively on Peacock andonly for paid subscribers. This time around, subscribers have gotten a lot more bang for their buck.

Whether you like it or not, itГўВҖВҷs a reality of our world that streaming platforms are becoming essential for sports fans who want to watch everything. Peacock had that NFL playoff game. Netflix will have the NFLГўВҖВҷs Christmas games. Apple TV+ has exclusive MLB windows. Amazon hasThursday Night Footballand just bought rights to the NBA.

There are still millions and millions of people who like to sit down and watch the prime-time broadcast on regular old NBC. But for those who want to see everything liveГўВҖВ”those taking advantage of modern work-from-home philosophies or those who simply tolerate more screen time than they did in 2016ГўВҖВ”Gold Zonehas been a game-changer.

As Iseman himself said, thereГўВҖВҷs no going back.

The Los Angeles Olympics in 2028 will be outstanding forGold Zoneand offer easy time slots to follow along. Brisbane, Australia, four years after that will be interesting, to see the appetite for overnightGold Zone. Some of the Winter Olympics may be interesting as well.

But now that weГўВҖВҷve gotten a taste ofGold Zoneat its best, the people who run it must find a way to make it work. They owe it to their country.

Mitch Goldich is a senior editor for Sports Illustrated, mostly focused on the NFL. He has also covered the Olympics extensively and written on a variety of sports since joining SI in 2014. His work has been published by The New York Times, Baseball Prospectus and Food & Wine, among other outlets. Goldich has a bachelorГўВҖВҷs in journalism from Lehigh University and a masterГўВҖВҷs in journalism from the Medill School at Northwestern University. He is a Philly native, now living in Boston, and he invented and popularized the octopus, when the same player scores a touchdown and the ensuing two-point conversion.

title: NBCГўВҖВҷs ГўВҖВҳGold ZoneГўВҖВҷ Has Perfected Live Olympics Coverage, and ThereГўВҖВҷs No Going Back
description: It was hard not to get hooked on the Summer Games in Paris this year, as the networkГўВҖВҷs streaming product provided a wide-ranging and compelling viewing experience.
